Thanks for the response @luthur. So you are saying Flower does not currently support starting a worker that has been shutdown? If so that's a shame. Would be a great feature.

@ask If someone were to continue implementing this; is this how you envision the flow?

0. Given CELERY_SEND_TASK_SENT_EVENT is enabled; together with a ALLOW_FAILED_TASK_RETRY toggle in flower.
1. Store/cache started task queue/exchange/routing-key as they arrive from `task-sent` event.
2. Store/cache original task args/kwargs (I may be wrong here, but they [appear to be included in `task-sent`](http://celery.readthedocs.org/en/latest/userguide/monitoring.html#task-sent)).
3. If task does not fail, remove the above stored data.
4. (optionally) allow cleaning up stored failed task metadata.
5. If user requests a retry, send `task.apply_async` (support `task.retry`?) to celery app.

While this satisfies our immediate requirement, it does feel to cover specific use cases.

However, now that I see that flower has the ability to schedule tasks, why not add an arbitrary task execution view? We could allow the task metadata (argument and routing) to be pre-filled from "saved instances" such as user defined or from previous tasks (i.e: filtered by status).

For the "retry" use case, I can envision users registering a task which handles task retries; the task, accepts task-ids as arguments, fetches task AsyncResult and retries task in the manner the user requires.

We obviously wouldn't be able to fit all use cases, but allowing arbitrary task execution and a dedicated "retry" task registered on user end, would allow us to forego the above steps (0-4) and still able to retry with the original data.

"Address already in use" issues can generally be cleaned up by avoiding hard-coding addresses in tests, or even better letting the test bind an arbitrary open port and then passing that into the client. This is kinda tiresome, but certainly doable. The `test_blocking` thing would need to be debugged, of course. Or you could probably make the test suite faster by going through and removing calls to `sleep`, etc.; again, tiresome and requires diligence, but doable. You can pass `--durations=10` to get pytest to list the slowest tests, which are a good place to start this kind of optimization.

In case it's any inspiration / competitive motivation, the trio test suite on my laptop currently takes 6.4 seconds to run 422 tests, single-threaded :-). (And with `-n auto` it passes, but takes 9.5 seconds... I guess the tests are so fast that the parallel scheduler overhead dominates.)

Should an application keep track of its own
subprocesses, threads, connections, http requests,
open files, etc, etc, and other resources? Yes ? May be? No?

What is different with its created tasks?

Other than this, there is quiet a bit of discussion for cyclic references
involving exception leaks on python. I guess you are making a general
projection on to Curio, rather than having caught aspecific
bug. If you have a caught a specific bug please eloborate
with a 'reproducable case'.

And please note that all such evil stems from
Garbage Collection/Tracking. Your proposal adds another
layer for it.

I think you've misunderstood the issue.
This is regarding the [`Packet.create` `sender`-input](https://github.com/kipe/enocean/blob/f6825c45161600ac3dec95c37a9e0b7a94ca300a/examples/enocean_example.py#L18). It's requested on [line 29](https://github.com/kipe/enocean/blob/f6825c45161600ac3dec95c37a9e0b7a94ca300a/examples/enocean_example.py#L29) from the module attached to your Raspberry Pi or the USB-dongle. (For documentation, see [EnOcean Serial Protocol 3](http://www.enocean.com/esp), page 34).

So in essence, the idea is not to limit the `destination` of `Packet.create`, just using the correct device ID, from which we're sending. So for example, you would set the `destination` to 0xFFD05081 or 0xFFD05082, and `sender` would be the ID of your transmitter/receiver module attached to your RasPi/PC.

@imphil Actually you can force Vivado to not to that:

1. create a new xdc file in the development tree
1. go to `sources` and right click on the xdc, then `Set as Target Constraint File`
1. voila, only this one is used now for the debug probes

You should really be linking to the system copy of libpng. Then if that build/run test fails you fall back to building the libpng in dlib/external.

Mapping floats to the range +/- 3.4e +/- 38 is a terrible idea.  Numerically, now if you do simple operations like difference pixels you will have the differencing do nothing since subtracting floating point values far apart in value like that is a noop (e.g. 1e30 - 1 == 1e30, which is not what you want).  It would break essentially any image processing algorithm when applied to such images.  

As for 16bit images, just because an image is 16 bits doesn't mean it has 16bits of information in it.  I've encountered plenty of imaging sensors in my career that output images with 13 or 14 bits.  These are naturally packed into uint16_t or something similar.  You certainly would not want to assume the max value was 65565, because it isn't.  You also wouldn't want to remap it to 0 to 65565.  A common reason to use higher bit images is to allow you to do arithmetic on an image that needs higher precision because of some aspect of the intervening calculation.  Maybe you are multiplying pixels by some number.  But if you already saturated the range by stuffing 8 bits into the 8 high order bits of a 16bit integer, now you have lost all room to create larger values.  Again, not what you want.

I could go on all day about how this is a bad idea and what kind of trouble you will run into if you try to do this in real image processing code.  Realistically, for images most people encounter, the range is always 0 to 255.  Some libraries stuff things into floats and in those cases it's generally 0 to 1.   But any time you are loading an image from disk it's almost certainly going to have pixels in the range 0 to 255 unless you are dealing with special sensors, which for most users is not the case, and even if it was, automatic scaling would just make your life harder.

I should also point out that I've encountered plenty of 16 bit image formats that, when you look at them, actually contain 8bit data.  What happens when you load that kind of image into an 8bit representation if there is automatic scaling?  Do all the pixels get set to 0 because the high order bits of the image are all 0?  Again, not what you want.

Hi Nathan. Thanks for the report. I agree this does sound problemmatic.

The [XDG spec](http://standards.freedesktop.org/basedir-spec/basedir-spec-latest.html) states that if $XDG_DATA_HOME isn't set, "a default equal to $HOME/.local/share should be used."

I should also point out that while keyring currently uses $XDG_DATA_HOME for both config and data, it should be using $XDG_CONFIG_HOME (see #99).

So I see several approaches here. The first, most obvious, and easiest would be to set the XDG environment variables to '.':

```
import os
base = '.'
os.environ['XDG_CONFIG_HOME'] = os.environ['XDG_DATA_HOME'] = base
```

That would work if your daemons run from a fixed working directory and don't share that working directory.

You could also set those paths to a daemon-specific directory, such as the virtualenv root:

```
import os
import sys
base = os.path.join(sys.prefix, 'etc')
os.environ['XDG_CONFIG_HOME'] = os.environ['XDG_DATA_HOME'] = base
```

Would either of those approaches work for you?

On looking at the snippet for my class Mesh posted in #175, I thought that perhaps a [`namedtuple`](https://docs.python.org/3/library/collections.html?highlight=namedtuple#collections.namedtuple) would have sufficed.

There had been some talk of using these to return multiple values in SciPy and related projects; see, e.g., scipy/scipy#3991.

Later, I think that that idea was been superseded by another generic container class called [`Bunch`](https://gist.github.com/ev-br/a11e5a25a98c2b0115ed), as discussed in [scipy#3665](https://github.com/scipy/scipy/issues/3665#issuecomment-106085454).

There are a mixture of behaviours in SciPy 1.0.0, with functions returning:
* multiple values (i.e. plain tuples)
* namedtuples
* objects like Bunch, e.g. [`scipy.optimize.OptimizeResult`](https://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/generated/scipy.optimize.OptimizeResult.html#scipy.optimize.OptimizeResult)

OptimizeResult subclasses `dict`.

Hahahaha LOL oh how much I agree with you!  So I'm open to suggestions, but I have learned that function objects can actually be pickled.  It's just that a detail about how Sphinx parses `conf.py` means that you cannot pickle a function defined in `conf.py` itself.  Meaning in the future with the planned writer interface sometime with 1.x, I should be able to let users take very fine-grained control over how things are generated.

What exactly do you want to change?  It will help me understand how / where Exhale can let you hook into things.

That said, there is this "secret" saving of the `ExhaleRoot` object that the testing framework actually relies on to make comparisons.

https://github.com/svenevs/exhale/blob/ab69386af9b36da3cbb90ad3f749612dc1bf2e0b/exhale/deploy.py#L428-L429

So I *think* you can do something like this in `conf.py`

```py
def my_postprocessing(app):
    exhale_root = app.exhale_root
    the_function = None
    for node in exhale_root.all_nodes:
        if node.kind == "function" and node.name == "function::you::wanna::change":
            the_function = node
            break

    if not the_function:
        raise RuntimeError("couldn't find it...")

    file_path = os.path.join(exhale_root.root_directory, the_function.file_name)
    # open / edit etc
```

I didn't test that but that should be close, but the order matters -- exhale has to go first, so I _think_ what you do is below that

```py
# in conf.py, it makes your `conf.py` an "extension"
def setup(app):
    app.setup_extension("exhale")
    app.connect("builder-inited", my_postprocessing)
```

What that should do is say "hey I need exhale to run first", then in the same event (`builder-inited`) run `my_postprocessing`.

Some small notes:

1. `app.exhale_root` will disappear in v1.0.0, it's undocumented because it was a temporary hack.
2. Note that you have to do `os.path.join(exhale_root.root_directory, something)`.  By the time you get access to `exhale_root` the regrettable function has been called (also going away in 1.0.0, this was a dumb thing for me to do...)

    https://github.com/svenevs/exhale/blob/ab69386af9b36da3cbb90ad3f749612dc1bf2e0b/exhale/graph.py#L3027-L3038

3. You only have to do that join for leaf documents (the ones the breathe directives are actually on).  The top-level documents for library root etc should be safe to just access these fields directly from `exhale_root`:

    https://github.com/svenevs/exhale/blob/ab69386af9b36da3cbb90ad3f749612dc1bf2e0b/exhale/graph.py#L908-L911
4. Don't get trapped by my extremely confused bookeeping variables.  By the time you get access, `exhale_root.class_like` does **not** contain all classes and structs!  This is a detail specific to making the tree view easier to generate.  It only contains top-level things by the time you get it.  So if you want to change all class / struct pages:

    ```py
    all_class_like = [node for node in exhale_root.all_nodes if node.kind in ["class", "struct"]]
    for node in all_class_like:
        file_path = os.path.join(exhale_root.root_directory, node.file_name)
        # do stuffs
    ```

Hope that makes sense.  Does this enable you to bypass what you want?

In my example the difference between `version.txt` and `mypkg/init.py` is that the former uses `'{{ major }}.{{ minor }}.{{ patch }}'` as a serializer (which is the pattern used to match and to replace the version) while the latter uses `'{{ major }}.{{ minor }}'`.

You have to remove `'env_checker/checker.py'` from the `FILES` dictionary, otherwise Punch will use on it both the `GLOBALS` serilizer (incorrectly changing the address) and the specialised `FILES` serializer (correctly changing the version).

I've done a quick evaluation and would suggest a few basic improvements:
1) addition of 'und' (undetermined) when really the word is not in any language
2) give weight to known stop words
3) give weight to punctuation
4) consider that certain characters only occur in certain languages
5) train on translit too to identify languages like Arabic, Russian, Hindi and Chinese when they are written in the Latin script

```
langid.classify("haha")
('en', 0.16946150595865334)
langid.classify("!!!")
('en', 0.16946150595865334)
langid.classify("no")
('en', 0.16946150595865334)
langid.classify("no!")
('en', 0.16946150595865334)
langid.classify("¡No!")
('zh', 0.2249412262395412)
```
The last should of course be Spanish, not Chinese.

```
langid.classify("yeah haha")
('id', 0.4730470342933074)
langid.classify("ты я меня так что да нет не же")
('bg', 0.6202337036529055)
```
Note, that is unambiguously Russian, not Bulgarian.  Most of the words and even one of the characters, 'ы', is unknown in Bulgarian.

```
langid.classify("jajaj")
('en', 0.16946150595865334)
langid.classify("jaja")
('en', 0.16946150595865334)
```
I think Spanish or German are more reasonable guesses here, using words or character-n-grams-based approaches.

```
langid.classify("ty kuda edish' seychas?")
('es', 0.48318636730355763)
```
This is actually Russian translit.

langid.classify("asdfk94jlskdle")
('en', 0.16946150595865334)
```
In my opinion, this should return '(\'und\', 0.99)'.

Thanks for the suggestion, but I think that's beyond the scope of WhiteNoise. In fact, the whole point of WhiteNoise is to provide simple static file handing regardless of how you choose to deploy your application, so it deliberately doesn't specify one particular method of deployment.

I'm not sure if using `runserver` in production is a good idea or not (it might be fine, but as far as I know it's intended more as a development server than a production one). I've tended to use gunicorn myself.

You're right that the Django docs on deployment haven't been updated to reflect the new tools we now have. But if you Google for "django docker" you should fine plenty of advice on deploying Django apps with Docker.

The benefit of WhiteNoise is that once you've got the wsgi app deployed and running you don't need to do anything _extra_ to get static files working.

Pretend for a moment that what is currently considered to be the "id" column was invisible, but that you also had a `.guid` column. If this guid column were auto-generating by default, but you could set to something explicitly if you chose, and you could get the object by the value, prefix, and suffix, would that suffice?

```
import uuid
import rom

class GuidPrimaryKeyExample(rom.Model):
    _id = rom.PrimaryKey()
    guid = rom.String(default=lambda:str(uuid.uuid4),
        unique=True, prefix=True, suffix=True)
```

Add columns and methods as necessary, and that's it. Now, there are *very* good reasons to use an integer primary key, even if it is hidden, among which being that if you have any indexes, your memory utilization can be as much as 1/4 as the size of a guid primary key. But this gets you the guid you want, with the integer primary key that rom needs to be efficient.

I think that's enough support. Do you agree?

You can pass your own factory function to return whatever you want. In this case, if you just want to pull the first column of data requested (regardless of how many there are):

```
def first_column_factory_function(columns):
    # ignore the columns
    def make(data):
        return data[0]
    return make
```

Then, if you want to get your unwrapped single column, you would:
```
for cv in Model.query.select('column', include_pk=False, ff=first_column_factory_function):
```

Pie charts are actually a special case. They can be thought of as having a single series.

In any case, you're right, there is no fill property implemented for Pie charts yet. When there is, it will have to be on individual points, not on the series, since each data point in the single pie series will need a contrasting color.

For either approach you'll be on your own in the short term, meaning you'll need to work with the python-pptx internals and lxml to build a workaround function to get what you want.

The z-order approach might be simpler. Essentially this is changing the document order of the shapes on a slide, so maybe something like this aircode:

```python
shapes = slide.shapes
old_picture = shapes[42]  # or whatever
new_picture = shapes.add_picture(...)
old_pic = old_picture._element
new_pic = new_picture._element
old_pic.addnext(new_pic)  # method on lxml _Element, moves in this case, doesn't copy
old_pic.getparent().remove(old_pic)
```

This isn't tested, but should give you an idea.

If you're transmitting over HTTP POST, your problem is likely the transmission of raw binary over a text protocol. I see a lot of `encode` and `decode` in your code. The purpose of these functions is to change binary data into text (e.g. in UTF-8). **It is not to prepare binary data for transport**, and encoding can result in a string that decodes legitimately differently when the same character occupies two code points. When transmitting binary data over text, use base64 or similar. In HTTP POST, you can also send MIME multipart (which is really just base64 encoding, but with more metadata).

As for your project, **BE VERY CAREFUL AT INCORRECTLY REIMPLEMENTING CRYPTOGRAPHIC PROTOCOLS**. Like the cryptographic primitives, incorrect implementation of cryptographic protocols can lead to less or worse security. If you want to identify a client to a server, that functionality already exists within TLS, see [Client Authenticated Handshake](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transport_Layer_Security#Client-authenticated_TLS_handshake).

Regarding the workflow for merging to `master`. I haven't figured that out completely yet, but this is what I do know should work quite well:
 1. Update version to new release in `dev` though PR or commit
 2. Locally
    1. Checkout a new feature branch from master (be sure to fetch the current master branch first)
    2. Merge `dev` into feature branch
 3. Create a PR from the feature branch
 4. `normal` merge the PR into the master branch. This will create a merge commit and GitHub will notice that now `master` is ahead of `dev`
 5. Therefore merge `master` into `dev` and push the commit to Github.
 6. Both branches should now be equal. And any new PR can be merged into dev.
 7. Create a new release on GitHub
 8. Lastly push the new version to `pypi`

I apologize for my delay in getting back on this issue. Here is some code (written for Python 3.4 and newer) which demonstrates the issue at hand, that plumbum `LocalMachine` can not be used safely for concurrent tasks:

```python
#!/usr/bin/env python
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

import asyncio

from plumbum.machines import LocalMachine


@asyncio.coroutine
def first_setter():
    local = LocalMachine()
    with local.env(PRIVATE_VAR='before'):
        print("PRIVATE_VAR of first_setter() before sleeping: {}".format(
              local.env['PRIVATE_VAR']))
        yield from asyncio.sleep(1)
        print("PRIVATE_VAR of first_setter() after sleeping: {}".format(
              local.env['PRIVATE_VAR']))


@asyncio.coroutine
def second_setter():
    local = LocalMachine()
    with local.env(PRIVATE_VAR='after'):
        print("PRIVATE_VAR of second_setter(): {}".format(
              local.env['PRIVATE_VAR']))
        yield from asyncio.sleep(3)


loop = asyncio.get_event_loop()
tasks = [asyncio.async(first_setter()), asyncio.async(second_setter())]
loop.run_until_complete(asyncio.wait(tasks))
loop.close()
```

Running the above script gives the following output:

```
PRIVATE_VAR of first_setter() before sleeping: before
PRIVATE_VAR of second_setter(): after
PRIVATE_VAR of first_setter() after sleeping: after
```

Here's what I as a user would expect:

```
PRIVATE_VAR of first_setter() before sleeping: before
PRIVATE_VAR of second_setter(): after
PRIVATE_VAR of first_setter() after sleeping: before
```

The work performed by `first_setter` should be completely isolated from the work in `second_setter`, much like how setting an environment variable in one shell session does not set that variable for a second shell session. As a user, I need a way to safely run multiple tasks concurrently using different contexts (environment variables, cwd, etc.). Letting `LocalMachine()` returns a, independent instance of `LocalMachine`, not the same instance — with its own instances of `LocalEnv`, `LocalWorkdir`, and any other objects that maintain state — would allow plumbum to be used concurrently in a safe manner.

```python
import xlsxwriter

workbook   = xlsxwriter.Workbook('rich_url.xlsx')

url_format = workbook.add_format({'color': 'blue', 'underline': 1})

worksheet.set_column('A:A', 50)

my_url = 'http://www.python.org'

# Write the URL to create a link.
worksheet.write_url('A3', my_url)

# Overwrite the string part of the URL with a rich string.
worksheet.write_rich_string('A3',
                            url_format, my_url,
                            ' is the home of python')

> Or a way to connect via the HTTP or SSL interface and issue commands?

Sure, you can manually connect to either the TCP or the SSL interface:

    nc $HOST 50001 # for TCP
    openssl s_client -connect $HOST:50002 # for SSL

You can then enter commands for the server. This returns the electrumx version:

    {"id": 0, "method": "server.version", "params": []}

This gives information about the server, for example the public hostname:

    {"id": 0, "method": "server.features", "params": []}

For more commands see https://github.com/kyuupichan/electrumx/blob/master/docs/PROTOCOL.rst

Most likely your issue is either:
 - incorrect configured public hostname
 - incorrect IP to bind on (e.g. `localhost`)
 - a firewall issue

  elif ('+' in san or '#' in san) != board.is_into_check(move):
    raise ValueError("{2} a check: {0} in {1}".format(repr(san), board.fen(), ("not" if '+' in san else "is")))
  # other cases that are not strictly valid:
  # * over-disambiguation: Ngf3
  # * promotions without =: e8Q
  # * incorrect #
  raise ValueError("san not strictly correct: {0} in {1}".format(repr(san), board.fen()))
```

Could we put this functionality on `rendezvous.AppNamespace` instead of on the websocket-specific frontend server? I've got two motivations:

* future frontends (non-websocket) should honor this setting too
* some apps will want to allow this, some won't. (in fact really it's likely to be that some nameplates will want themselves to be tab-completable, and some won't, like offline/human-generated wormhole codes). So I'd like to move this closer to the nameplate record, and putting it in `AppNamespace` is as close as we can get without actually changing the DB schema yet.

Basically we want `AppNamespace.get_nameplate_ids` to return an empty list if nameplate-listing has been disabled. We need to rename the existing function to `_get_nameplate_ids` so it can continue to be used by `_find_available_nameplate_id` (which needs it to do allocation). Then the new `get_nameplate_ids()` will just be a wrapper that checks the permission flag and then either returns `self._get_nameplate_ids()` or `[]`. The permission flag should be passed down into the `AppNamespace` through `Rendezvous.get_app()`, so the `WebSocketRendezvousFactory` shouldn't see it at all.

If/when we make individual nameplates listable or not, I think we'll use an AND check. `_get_nameplate_ids()` will return an empty list if the app permission is turned off, else it will return just the nameplates that the DB record says are listable.

We've talked about allowing *allocation* to be enabled on a per-frontend basis, which would make some of these feature/permission switches want to live on a Factory, but I think I like our idea about API tokens better (so a co-located specially-blessed "client" uses its secret token to perform allocation, rather than using a special network socket that's unreachable from anywhere else).

Also, could you squash this down into one commit? Thanks!

From the OP:

> However, if typing is no longer part of the stdlib, I guess it's possible for another module named typing to be used in its place, and then I need to be more defensive about looking inside sys.modules['typing']. Is that case worth worrying about? I sort of think it's not, although it would be easy enough to add a getattr(typing, 'ClassVar') to the code.

*If* we remove `typing` from the stdlib (which is less and less likely given the complications and worries) I would put that bit of defensive coding in, just so that if someone does create their own `typing` module and it doesn't have `ClassVar` their use of `dataclasses` (without using `ClassVar` obviously) will not fail mysteriously. Especially since this is only one tiny bit of code. (I'd add a comment too.) If `typing` stays in the stdlib I wouldn't worry (in general we almost never worry about users sabotaging the stdlib).

> The other thing to worry about is: what if typing is removed, but something in the stdlib wants to have a dataclass with a ClassVar?

I don't like any of the schemes proposed for this in this thread. They all seem way too harebrained, and would require mypy to jump through extra hoops. *If* typing is removed, then stdlib modules had better not try to use `ClassVar`. (In fact stdlib modules had better be conservative with using `dataclasses` for one full release -- `dataclasses` will be approved provisionally (but without any of the [nonsense](https://bugs.python.org/issue31742) about emitting `FutureWarning` every time it's imported).

Hey, thanks for reporting this issue! 

This is caused by the dependency detection code for functions, which uses simple string matching to determine the dependencies (we have to resort to this hacky approach because Postgres doesn't track function dependencies directly). 

For instance if `function1` appears on the definition of `function2` then `function1` is assumed to be a dependency of `function2`.

In most cases this works OK, and gives `migra` the power to manage functions better. The downside is you sometimes get problems like this, where there are false positive dependencies detected and you get circular references. We probably need to add some better error handling for this scenario, or the ability to turn off this tracking if desired.

Turns out I had an issue at two different levels:

 1) [Django REST Framework](https://github.com/tomchristie/django-rest-framework) (DRF) was not requiring the version (even though it was required in the model) and then was "filling it in" with the value from the database.  Obviously, this broke the concurrency check.

I've submitted a [ticket](https://github.com/tomchristie/django-rest-framework/issues/4231) at DRF and worked around this issue in my local copy.  The logical fix from the perspective of the REST framework was to, at minimum, use the default value (if available) for any value absent in the PUT.  The default for these fields is `version=0`.  Unfortunately, that leads to the second issue:

 2) django-concurrency sees the default value and skips version checks [here](https://github.com/saxix/django-concurrency/blob/1.2/src/concurrency/fields.py#L191)

I'm about to submit a PR that:

 - Creates a settings flag IGNORE_DEFAULT=True
 - Adapts the field logic to check the version unless this flag is enabled

I also had to account for an edge case (documented inline) that can occur during a create.  This required a change in the way the logic flowed.

I added a test that failed under the old code.  All of the old tests pass except one.  As I'll reiterate in the PR, I'm not clear why the test should fail to fix the code.  If you can explain, I'll update the PR accordingly.

Looking at the code, I think the casting to type is already being done (for the most part, maybe not date/date-time), it just needs to be added to the request somewhere.  With gluapy I added a dict at `request.data` where the keys were the parameter names, and the values were the already casted python values.  I liked that setup a lot and would be interested in doing the same here.

`request.data` could be some other property, maybe `request.swagger_data` ? Parameter names are unique so there should be no chance of conflicts in the dict.

I like this setup because you still get access to the raw request values in their usual place, and you don't have to deal with extracting values from different parts of the request object. They're all available in the same dict.

If fact, exit code is not handled: the return value of ``artifact.ArtifactCLI().run()`` is not used except in tests (or may be in client code – if any exist). So you need to add an explicit ``sys.exit(0)``.

Why it returns always *1*? Because of the definition of the entry point (see ``bin/artifact`` in your virtualenv):

```python
__requires__ = 'repositorytools'
import sys
from pkg_resources import load_entry_point

if __name__ == '__main__':
    sys.exit(
        load_entry_point('repositorytools', 'console_scripts', 'artifact')()
    )
```

The script calls ``artifact.ArtifactCLI().run()`` and use the return value as an exit code.

If it returns an integer, you'll get this exit code (module 128 and a sign).

```bash
$ python -c "import sys; sys.exit(0);"
$ echo $?
0

$ python -c "import sys; sys.exit(12);"
$ echo $?
12
```

But if the return value is not an integer you'll always get 1 (and a message is displayed).

```bash
$ python -c "import sys; sys.exit('bye.');"
bye.
$ echo $?
1
```

To make the entry points works, you need to return 0 in the ``repositorytools.cli.common.CLI.__call__`` method:

```python
    def __call__(self, *args):
        self.run(*args)
        return 0  # exit code
```

After looking through the script, I feel it's not the problem of `asnumpy()`. The mxnet operators are async functions in python, which means they may be still running in backend (C++) while python main thread moves to other steps. I suspect that the bottleneck is in the nested loops where ndarray slicing and slicing with assignment are triggered frequently. You can use `tpreds.wait_to_read()` to setup a barrier in the python thread to force the execution on `tpreds` to finish in backend before moving forward in frontend and time the execution.

I think that raising a python exception in case of not 200 responses is reasonable since those type of responses means errors. Adding a config parameter could be a solution but it will open us to maintain a bigger number of different flows, so I would say that we should not change it. Obviously if a community will require that we might start thinking about it.

About your test case I'm making the following assumptions
 - ``bravado`` is a fixture that gives you a ``bravado.client.SwaggerClient`` object
 - ``bravado`` is configured with ``also_return_response``
 - is defined an endpoint that will be retrieved via ``bravado.resource.patch_resource_id```
 - a running service will respond to the requests
what are ``resource_id``, ``payload`` and ``user_token``? I'm assuming again that are somehow valid python variables that will be valid according to the swagger specs

Said so the easiest approach that I have in mind is to do something like
```python
@pytest.mark.parametrize("test_name, user_name", [
    ('200_action1', 'Tester_1'),
    ('400_action1', 'Tester_2'),
    ('200_action2', 'Tester_2'),
    ('401_action2', 'Tester_1')
])
def test_get_devices(bravado, test_name, user_name):
    raised_exception = None
    response = None
    try:
        # Get payload from files, user token and resource id. bravado is a fixture
        resource, response = bravado.resource.patch_resource_id(id=resource_id, resourceProperties=payload,token=user_token).result()
    except bravado.exceptionHttpError as e:
        raised_exception = e
    if test_name.startswith('200_'):
        assert raised_exception is None
    else:
        assert raised_exception is not None
        response = raised_exception.response
    
    assert response.status_code == int(test_name.split('_')[0])
    assert json.loads(response.text) == expected_body
```
Note I never tested the code ... so it could not work ... but I hope you get the idea :D

This is the rewrite rule on apache:

    ProxyPassReverse /i http://localhost:7029/

I need _vh_ because we need to append "/i/" to the url before Plone routes.
For example calling _https://mydomain.com/i/content-id_ should refer to _/siteid/content-id_ on Plone.

I'm a bit confused about how subrequest should works.
Trying to debug it deeper, i've found that other resources that calls subrequest (like rules.xml from [p.a.theming](https://github.com/plone/plone.app.theming/blob/master/src/plone/app/theming/utils.py#L127)) pass an url like this:

    /++theme++my.theme/rules.xml

and subrequest generates a path for subrequest like this:
    
    /VirtualHostBase/https/mydomain.com:443/siteid/VirtualHostRoot/_vh_i//++theme++my.theme/rules.xml

that fetches the right resource.

For tiles the things are different. [p.a.blocks](https://github.com/plone/plone.app.blocks/blob/master/plone/app/blocks/utils.py#L117) calls subrequest passing an url that could be:
- a complete url
- a path (in this case it prepends the site path)

I tried to set both of them to data attribute (data-tile) in my div. 
Setting the complete url, p.a.blocks calls subrequest with this url:

    https://mydomain.com/i/content-id/@@tile.type/tileid

and generates a path like this:

    /VirtualHostBase/https/mydomain.com:443/siteid/VirtualHostRoot/_vh_i/i/content-id/@@tile.type/tileid

on second case (with this path: content-id/@@tile.type/tile_id), p.a.blocks add siteid to the path, and calls subrequest with this url:

    /siteid/content-id/@@tile.type/tile_id

that generates a path like this:

    /VirtualHostBase/https/mydomain.com:443/siteid/VirtualHostRoot/_vh_i/siteid/content-id/@@tile.type/tileid

both of them are wrong and subrequest can't find the resource.

Maybe an optional boolean `force` argument, that will make `PGPKey.decrypt` try to decrypt all PKESKs with thrown keyids if the keyid is not found in any PKESK. It will then try to decrypt the message with the gained session keys, and if all goes well (meaning no exceptions) it will return the decrypted message. This of course doesn't mean that the decrypted message was successfully decrypted, but it is something, and possibly the most we can do one the "message" decryption layer. Yep, your assumption is correct. When doing `iam.roles.all()` we'll call `ListRoles` to populate all of the `Role` objects. However, this API isn't returning the `permission_boundary` field and that's why you have to do a `role.load()` if you want to populate that field via a `GetRole` API call. For what it's worth, the `ListRoles` operation is documented and modeled as returning a roles but I'm not sure if omitting this field was intentional or an oversight. I'll forward this along to the service team. For now, you'll have to use the `role.load()` work around as that's the only option on the client side.

One way to get to these attributes is through the RouteTable's  [association collection](https://boto3.readthedocs.io/en/latest/reference/services/ec2.html#EC2.RouteTable.associations). So something like this:
```python
import boto3
rt = boto3.resource("ec2").RouteTable("id")
for rta in rt.associations.all():
    print(rta.subnet_id)
```

```
# -*- coding: utf-8 -*-

import colander
import colander.interfaces


__version__ = '0.2'


class ConversionError(Exception):
    pass


class NoSuchConverter(ConversionError):
    pass


def convert_length_validator_factory(max_key, min_key):
    """
    :type max_key: str
    :type min_key: str
    """
    def validator_converter(schema_node, validator):
        """
        :type schema_node: colander.SchemaNode
        :type validator: colander.interfaces.Validator
        :rtype: dict
        """
        converted = None
        if isinstance(validator, colander.Length):
            converted = {}
            if validator.max is not None:
                converted[max_key] = validator.max
            if validator.min is not None:
                converted[min_key] = validator.min
        return converted
    return validator_converter


def convert_oneof_validator_factory(null_values=(None,)):
    """
    :type null_values: iter
    """
    def validator_converter(schema_node, validator):
        """
        :type schema_node: colander.SchemaNode
        :type validator: colander.interfaces.Validator
        :rtype: dict
        """
        converted = None
        if isinstance(validator, colander.OneOf):
            converted = {}
            converted['enum'] = list(validator.choices)
            if not schema_node.required:
                converted['enum'].extend(list(null_values))
        return converted

    return validator_converter


def convert_range_validator(schema_node, validator):
    """
    :type schema_node: colander.SchemaNode
    :type validator: colander.interfaces.Validator
    :rtype: dict
    """
    converted = None
    if isinstance(validator, colander.Range):
        converted = {}
        if validator.max is not None:
            converted['maximum'] = validator.max
        if validator.min is not None:
            converted['minimum'] = validator.min
    return converted


def convert_regex_validator(schema_node, validator):
    """
    :type schema_node: colander.SchemaNode
    :type validator: colander.interfaces.Validator
    :rtype: dict
    """
    converted = None
    if isinstance(validator, colander.Regex):
        converted = {}
        if hasattr(colander, 'url') and validator is colander.url:
            converted['format'] = 'uri'
        elif isinstance(validator, colander.Email):
            converted['format'] = 'email'
        else:
            converted['pattern'] = validator.match_object.pattern
    return converted


class ValidatorConversionDispatcher(object):

    def __init__(self, *converters):
        self.converters = converters

    def __call__(self, schema_node, validator=None):
        """
        :type schema_node: colander.SchemaNode
        :type validator: colander.interfaces.Validator
        :rtype: dict
        """
        if validator is None:
            validator = schema_node.validator
        converted = {}
        if validator is not None:
            for converter in (self.convert_all_validator,) + self.converters:
                ret = converter(schema_node, validator)
                if ret is not None:
                    converted = ret
                    break
        return converted

    def convert_all_validator(self, schema_node, validator):
        """
        :type schema_node: colander.SchemaNode
        :type validator: colander.interfaces.Validator
        :rtype: dict
        """
        converted = None
        if isinstance(validator, colander.All):
            converted = {}
            for v in validator.validators:
                ret = self(schema_node, v)
                converted.update(ret)
        return converted


class TypeConverter(object):

    type = ''
    convert_validator = lambda self, schema_node: {}

    def __init__(self, dispatcher):
        """
        :type dispatcher: TypeConversionDispatcher
        """
        self.dispatcher = dispatcher

    def convert_type(self, schema_node, converted):
        """
        :type schema_node: colander.SchemaNode
        :type converted: dict
        :rtype: dict
        """
        converted['type'] = self.type
        if not schema_node.required:
            converted['type'] = [converted['type'], 'null']
        if schema_node.title:
            converted['title'] = schema_node.title
        if schema_node.description:
            converted['description'] = schema_node.description
        if schema_node.default is not colander.null:
            converted['default'] = schema_node.default
        return converted

    def __call__(self, schema_node, converted=None):
        """
        :type schema_node: colander.SchemaNode
        :type converted: dict
        :rtype: dict
        """
        if converted is None:
            converted = {}
        converted = self.convert_type(schema_node, converted)
        converted.update(self.convert_validator(schema_node))
        return converted


class BaseStringTypeConverter(TypeConverter):

    type = 'string'
    format = None

    def convert_type(self, schema_node, converted):
        """
        :type schema_node: colander.SchemaNode
        :type converted: dict
        :rtype: dict
        """
        converted = super(BaseStringTypeConverter,
                          self).convert_type(schema_node, converted)
        if schema_node.required:
            converted['minLength'] = 1
        if self.format is not None:
            converted['format'] = self.format
        return converted


class BooleanTypeConverter(TypeConverter):
    type = 'boolean'


class DateTypeConverter(BaseStringTypeConverter):
    format = 'date'


class DateTimeTypeConverter(BaseStringTypeConverter):
    format = 'date-time'


class NumberTypeConverter(TypeConverter):
    type = 'number'
    convert_validator = ValidatorConversionDispatcher(
        convert_range_validator,
        convert_oneof_validator_factory(),
    )


class IntegerTypeConverter(NumberTypeConverter):
    type = 'integer'


class StringTypeConverter(BaseStringTypeConverter):
    convert_validator = ValidatorConversionDispatcher(
        convert_length_validator_factory('maxLength', 'minLength'),
        convert_regex_validator,
        convert_oneof_validator_factory(('', None)),
    )


class TimeTypeConverter(BaseStringTypeConverter):
    format = 'time'


class ObjectTypeConverter(TypeConverter):

    type = 'object'

    def convert_type(self, schema_node, converted):
        """
        :type schema_node: colander.SchemaNode
        :type converted: dict
        :rtype: dict
        """
        converted = super(ObjectTypeConverter,
                          self).convert_type(schema_node, converted)
        properties = {}
        required = []
        for sub_node in schema_node.children:
            properties[sub_node.name] = self.dispatcher(sub_node)
            if sub_node.required:
                required.append(sub_node.name)
        converted['properties'] = properties
        if len(required) > 0:
            converted['required'] = required
        return converted


class ArrayTypeConverter(TypeConverter):

    type = 'array'
    convert_validator = ValidatorConversionDispatcher(
        convert_length_validator_factory('maxItems', 'minItems'),
    )

    def convert_type(self, schema_node, converted):
        """
        :type schema_node: colander.SchemaNode
        :type converted: dict
        :rtype: dict
        """
        converted = super(ArrayTypeConverter,
                          self).convert_type(schema_node, converted)
        converted['items'] = self.dispatcher(schema_node.children[0])
        return converted


class TypeConversionDispatcher(object):

    converters = {
        colander.Boolean: BooleanTypeConverter,
        colander.Date: DateTypeConverter,
        colander.DateTime: DateTimeTypeConverter,
        colander.Float: NumberTypeConverter,
        colander.Integer: IntegerTypeConverter,
        colander.Mapping: ObjectTypeConverter,
        colander.Sequence: ArrayTypeConverter,
        colander.String: StringTypeConverter,
        colander.Time: TimeTypeConverter,
    }

    def __init__(self, converters=None):
        """
        :type converters: dict
        """
        if converters is not None:
            self.converters.update(converters)

    def __call__(self, schema_node):
        """
        :type schema_node: colander.SchemaNode
        :rtype: dict
        """
        schema_type = schema_node.typ
        schema_type = type(schema_type)
        converter_class = self.converters.get(schema_type)
        if converter_class is None:
            raise NoSuchConverter
        converter = converter_class(self)
        converted = converter(schema_node)
        return converted


def finalize_conversion(converted):
    """
    :type converted: dict
    :rtype: dict
    """
    converted['$schema'] = 'http://json-schema.org/draft-04/schema#'
    return converted


def convert(schema_node, converters=None):
    """
    :type schema_node: colander.SchemaNode
    :type converters: dict
    :rtype: dict
    """
    dispatcher = TypeConversionDispatcher(converters)
    converted = dispatcher(schema_node)
    converted = finalize_conversion(converted)
    return converted
```

Hmm, interesting approach in #71. That certainly solves the ambiguity, but at the cost of making it impossible to use the `TypeRewriter` dispatch machinery for any custom type; you'd have to just use `isinstance` checks in `generic_rewrite` instead. That definitely wasn't the design intent. But it might be the best compromise we've got.

The other option would be to totally break back-compat of `TypeRewriter` and fully fix the issue. A couple ways I can think of to do that:

1. Change the method naming convention to `rewrite_module_typname` (where we'd  have to transform dots in the module name into underscores), so e.g. the existing `rewrite_Dict` would become `rewrite_typing_Dict`.

2. Use a metaclass/decorator combo instead, to explicitly register methods for rewrite dispatch, so you'd do something like `@rewrite('typing', 'Dict')` over your method def to explicitly say what it's supposed to rewrite.

My inclination is that we should do one of these (I lean slightly towards 2, since 1 still has the remote possibility of collision, and once we're fixing that possibility, we may as well fully fix it), rather than limiting type rewriters to operate only on builtin types.

```python

    'botocore>=1.8.0, <=1.8.21',
```

And `boto3==1.6.11` which [pins](https://github.com/boto/boto3/blob/1.6.11/setup.py):

```python
install_requires = [
    'botocore>=1.9.11,<1.10.0',
````

So I just checked on the dependency graph and indeed it can't be resolved, so you'll have to pin a compatible version yourself as they explicitly pin incompatible versions.

How did I check this? Well it says to run `pipenv install --skip-lock` followed by `pipenv graph`, which is what I did, and I saw straight away that they requested packages have explicit dependencies on conflicting versions of `botocore`.

While there might exist a version of boto3 that doesn't have an incompatible pin, when your pipfile can't be resolved pipenv doesn't crawl the entire resolved graph and download every wheel for every version of every package, check its `setup.py` and so on to make sure there are no conflicts, this would be highly impractical and would take an eternity.  Instead we download the packages you specify, resolve their dependencies, and, if they don't have strict pins, we find the ones that satisfy all dependencies for all packages.

In this case that isn't possible.  So now the onus is on you to pin one.  We don't get into the business of deciding which versions you meant to install. Explicit is better than implicit.

One option I was thinking about was to always go via [regions.RectanglePixelRegion](http://astropy-regions.readthedocs.io/en/latest/api/regions.RectanglePixelRegion.html) which does support floats, and then access it's `bounding_box` to do what `_from_float` does now.

But I don't actually think it's a good suggestion. `RectanglePixelRegion` has data members of center and width / height, not min / max of corners, and it can have an angle.

There's several other possible solutions: allow float in bounding box init, and add a method or attribute that goes to int index. Or have a separate class for float bounding boxes. Or mostly keep as-is and try to find a good name.

I don't know which of these options would be best here, or have a suggestion for a good name.

Quick answer re the choice of whether or not to support IMS/IUMS: afaik that is not up to you as spec authors, because your spec builds on http (which defines lots of things, including these headers).

So it should be left to the server implementer to either obey such headers, or always just execute the action / reply with the body anyway. This means clients can't rely on it, so in practice it will not be used a lot. But explicitly forbidding people to implement it would be subsetting http, which is bad practice afaik.

So you can choose not to talk about it, or mention it as a 'MAY'. (haven't looked yet if this is what you did).

Good catch about the plugins. I guess we have to bump the major version in Kinto because we expose the  underlying `cliquet` version to plugins.

I like the package name `kinto.core` better than `kinto_core`, but wanted to keep the repos separate so that someone could only depend on the smaller one if they were building a service. (I didn't pursue trying to do any kind of import magic to make it live in a separate repo but under the package path `kinto.core`.) But now that you mention it, I wonder if that's valuable. What kinds of things would other services use? How much weight is added by the rest of the Kinto repository?

Looking at the `kinto` repository, I see:

- `kinto/__main__.py`: a main script
- `kinto/__init__.py`: a main WSGI app
- `kinto/authorization.py`: permissions inheritance code
- `kinto/events.py`: definition of the ServerFlushed event
- `kinto/plugins/default_bucket`: just one plugin
- `kinto/config`: code to write a default config to disk
- `kinto/views`: the actual Kinto code

Altogether, we're looking at something like 700 lines of code plus tests (on top of cliquet's 4600 plus tests). Most of that code is probably not relevant to most kinto-based sites, but there isn't so much of it, so maybe it's worth the gains in simplicity.

You raised a good question. In the upload process we need to deal with missing _p_-values. My point is that the N/A value does not imply _p_ = 1. In fact, if you set `cooksCutoff=FALSE`, you'll get p < 1 for the majority of such genes. _p_ values near 1 mean that expressions of a gene in case and control group are very similar in relation to the error size. On the other hand, _p_ = N/A implies that expressions of a gene across samples don't fit well to negative binomial distribution. Consequently, error size may be mis-estimated which propagates to incorrectly computed _p_-value. However, _p_ = N/A doesn't say anything about the level of similarity of expressions between case and control group, as _p_ = 1 does. That's why I don't favor this approach.

To me, the eqivalent question is what to do, if e.g. _FDR_ = N/A. I would eliminate such genes from any further consideration and issue a warning already in the upload process.

Whatever the final solution, I just want to make sure that we are well aware of scientific consequences of the solution.

@phobologic I can think of quite a few different ways to move forward, some more pragmatic than others. I think all of them are already would already major improvement to the status quo.

a) Add `pre_build`, `post_build`, `pre_destroy` and `post_destroy` optional methods to blueprints and encourage people to move hook code there when applicable.  That would be the best fit with how I use most of the hooks, which is to provide capabilities that CFN omits or do local work to generate data required for a template. Handling Lambda functions, for example, would be much easier like this.
b) Add a `hooks` option at the top-level. Mostly re-use the current `Hook` definition, but resolve outputs in the arguments, and add a `requires` key. Internally merge their definitions in the DAG so everything runs neatly. Possibly deprecate `pre_build` and `post_build`.
c) Leave hooks as is, and provide a framework for easily writing and deploying custom CloudFormation resources instead. Would be a large project and completely different from the current usage patterns, but would be the "ideal" solution for many cases, and provide major benefits that hooks can never achieve, such as non-incorruptible deployments, rollbacks, independence from Stacker, etc.

I'm personally very interested in `c`, but I recognize it might not be feasible in the short term. Given how Terraform has been (justifiably) getting increasingly popular, I feel Stacker can stay relevant by playing to CloudFormation's strengths that TF can't match (mostly regarding deployment reliability), instead of moving closer to TF (by doing more work locally)

Hi Michael, we're always happy to have help with things, the main question is whether you have the skills (or, like I was initially, the willingness to develop them quickly :). It's a pretty big library by many people's standards, and fairly complex, so it takes some time to get oriented. Also we maintain pretty high engineering standards in order to keep the library robust at this size with part-time attention.

If none of that scares you away I recommend reading through this full thread and its predecessor #264 and see what you can learn about what it takes. After that you would probably might want to pick a different feature because the coordination overhead would be fairly high and it would be easy to get in each other's way (it's a fairly linear process within a particular feature), but I'll leave that call to @eupharis.

@ghcs27 I think we could import both modules if we prefer that approach. Each module instantiates an instance of EventListener. If one imported both modules, I think one instance of EventListener would be dormant. I suppose that in general I try to avoid that sort of outcome. 

The other alternative would be to add the following to `soco.config` (instead of what's in my previous message):

```
from .events import Subscription
EVENT_SUBSCRIPTION = Subscription
```

If one then did this:

```
import soco
from soco.events_twisted import Subscription
soco.config.EVENT_SUBSCRIPTION = Subscription
```

then the outcome would be much the same for the twisted user, except that `soco.events` would also have been imported and a dormant instance of `soco.events.EventListener` would have been created. I guess that's OK.

After looking at some source code of requests/openssl/cryptography, I think the problem is caused because the cryptography bundled inside Kolibri is not compatible with the OpenSSL in ubuntu's system, meaning that the cryptography inside Kolibri (version: 2.0.3) seems to be "too new" for the OpenSSL(version: 0.15.1) in ubuntu.

The problem seems to be related to https://github.com/pyca/cryptography/blob/10041cd9945ed33fd96788f5c620a58993d3926a/src/_cffi_src/openssl/ssl.py#L579.

Sorry I forgot to take a screenshot, but when we use kolibri's cryptography,  `Cryptography_HAS_SSL_ST`'s value is 0, `Cryptography_HAS_TLS_ST` is 1, and variable `SSL_ST_INIT` is not found. When we use the system's cryptography (version: 1.2.3),  variable `SSL_ST_INIT` exists and has some value.

> That's something I added today, since now there are now methods that assume that. I'm not sure it was required or even encouraged before. I believe I was usually doing the opposite, but it's easily changed.

The version of the file that I began hacking on already had the notion of V1 being higher in the docstring (see e.g., https://github.com/cuthbertLab/music21/blob/7319095d13896a64df7d2dc805c862086c121dcd/music21/voiceLeading.py - line 73), but the only functions that depend on the ordering of the voices are isProperResolution(), opensIncorrectly(), closesIncorrectly(), etc.

I'm wary of automatically detecting which voice is higher for the routines that depend on strict ordering, as it seems like there's a lot of room in that kind of scenario for user error to result in incorrect results that are difficult to catch. For example, a user might iterate through a two-voice counterpoint looking for incorrect resolutions, and instead of flagging a voice crossed segment as incorrect, an automatically-detecting-the-top-voice solution would flip the order in which it's considering the voices, potentially (and silently) giving unexpected/incorrect results.

A more aggressive solution could be that VLQ remains agnostic to which voice is the higher voice, but routines that require a strict ordering of voices throw AssertionErrors with malformed input. It's more hostile but less likely to produce unexpected bugs for users.

Actually, as it turns out, the following amounts to a logical _and_; not an _or_:

```
[X-Fleet]
MachineMetadata="controllerEligible=true"
MachineMetadata="deisRole=general"
```

The only way to achieve a logical or is by naming the _same_ key twice with different values on different lines as in the following example, directly from CoreOS documentation:

```
[X-Fleet]
MachineMetadata=region=us-east-1
MachineMetadata=region=us-west-1
```

Unfortunately, these limitations make it difficult, if not impossible to provide `MachineMetadata` that (for example) logically amounts to "run where controllerEligible=true or deisRole=general".

Does this mean we'd have to sacrifice the notion of a "general purpose" role?  Doing so would mean `user-data.example` would need lengthy list of metadata that explicitly makes each node eligible to run each component and that would have to be updated in the future if new components are added to Deis.

Anyone have ideas on a better way to do all this that I may be overlooking?

True

False

True

The thing is, the problems you described with `install.packages()` on Linux are not to do with `conda`. You need to install toolchains and system packages (when using `conda` via `conda install`, when using some other Linux distro via `apt-get install` or `dnf install`) so I don't think 'local installs of R outside of conda' will be any easier, ok, you might already have those toolchains and system packages installed on *your* system but that just leads to non-explicit dependency management (which conda always avoids) and problems when people without those system packages (or different versions of them) try to repeat what you built.

For managing R packages on Linux, my opinion is that CRAN is worse than conda because at least with conda we have bug-fixed and built 340 odd R packages as binaries in a very compatible way so that you do not have to.

My guess was correct: the reason 2.12.0 was not being selected was because it has missing dependencies; for instance, `flask-wtf==0.8.4`. The `binstar` channel has this dependency, but `binstar\channel\dev` does not.

I verified this by dropping `--no-deps`. And the following command allowed me to install version `2.12.0`, which lives only in the `dev` channel:
```
conda create -n test -c binstar -c binstar/channel/dev binstar-server=2.12.0
```

The fact is that `--no-deps` *still requires dependencies to be satisfiable*, even though it doesn't install them. In fact, when the `--no-deps` argument is supplied, `conda` generates a full solution as if `--no-deps` isn't there, then it drops the dependencies from the install plan.

At first I thought that this is clearly incorrect behavior. After all, a user specifying `--no-deps` is presumably aware that installing a package without its dependencies will produce an unusable installation. There is tacit acknowledgement that the user is about to break things.

On the other hand, whether the user realizes it or not, s/he *is* expecting conda to resolve the dependencies *to the extent that it can*. For instance, I would suggest that the user would consider it to be conda's fault if it installed a Python 2.x package in a Python 3.x environment or vice versa. If `--no-deps` truly ignored dependencies, it could sometimes do just that.

So clearly, `--no-deps` is problematic, and it's not clear to me what the solution is here.

Sorry for digging into the old issue, but I just want to add my 50 cents here.

> compression makes things slower

It is only true when the bottleneck is in client code. But in case of limited network capacity between bulk indexing clients and the ES cluster it is crucial to use the compression, which could reduce the size of bulk index requests by something like 10 times in many workloads related to logs and metrics, or 2 times in worst cases (it is still the json with its repeat-every-key-each-time syntax).

But anyway, if one wants to get around the client code bottleneck in bulk indexing process, then it would be a good idea to switch from python to another language, or at least keep away from elasticsearch-py which makes things more complicated than they are - I mean its http client and encoder abstractions with their default implementations, different bulk operation helpers and related configuration hastle. It would took 20 lines of python code to get a clean implementation of the bulk indexing just for some concrete case, and it could reduce the python code overhead at least by 50%. Or it could be 50 lines of golang code, which would reduce the CPU usage by another 50% or more, depending on document composition logic.

So I'd basically agree that it is really ok to have no parameter for client-side compression here.

What confused me was how my RAM could remain maxed after I would delete objects (del object) after being done with them, to the point that the only variable I see of any remarkable size is my dataframe of interest at 2.7GB based on df.info(memory_usage='deep'). I was using this function (found online from stack overflow, slightly altered) to try and see what could possibly be using up so much memory. ``` def check_mem(): # These are the usual ipython objects, including this one you are creating ipython_vars = ['In', 'Out', 'exit', 'quit', 'get_ipython', 'ipython_vars'] # Get a sorted list of the objects and their sizes print(sorted([(x, sys.getsizeof(globals().get(x))) for x in globals() if not x.startswith('_') and x not in sys.modules and x not in ipython_vars], key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True)) ``` Output of this was ``` [('examine', 2907181481), ('CategoricalDtype', 1056), ('NamespaceMagics', 888), ('month_dict', 648), ('files', 192), ('var_dic_list', 136), ('tqdm_notebook', 136), ('check_mem', 136), ('date_cols', 96), ('data_path', 92), ('pd', 80), ('np', 80), ('plt', 80), ('dc', 80), ('getsizeof', 72), ('filename', 71)] ``` 'examine' is my dataframe of interest. WOOT I figured it out!  So for me, `pytest_runtest_call` is "too late", since the exception occurs during the sphinx marker.  I don't think you can have it both ways, but I think in general `pytest_runtest_call` would be the average use case.  I will include my changes here without a PR because I don't know why I had to change some of them...

1. I had to use `pytest_runtest_setup`, not `pytest_runtest_call`.  Note that exceptions raised in the test body (if applicable) will now need to be used with traditional `with pytest.raises(...)`.
2. Probably related to changing (1), I would get attribute errors (unable to set) for the `outcome.excinfo = ...`.  Though probably not reliable, `__dict__` crawling revealed I *can* set `_excinfo`.
3. This is probably the only one you want to include here.  In the `else` clause at the bottom, doing `raise raised_exception` and only `except`ing on an `ExpectedException` is problematic.  This will crash `pytest` completely, rather than letting the test "gracefully fail".  My solution was to `elif raised_exception` with a slightly different message ;)

Here is the diff I am using and just added to my local `conftest.py`

```diff
diff --git a/pytest_raises/pytest_raises.py b/pytest_raises/pytest_raises.py
index 3987ea1..18e73f7 100644
--- a/pytest_raises/pytest_raises.py
+++ b/pytest_raises/pytest_raises.py
@@ -13,7 +13,7 @@ class ExpectedMessage(Exception):
 
 
 @pytest.hookimpl(hookwrapper=True)
-def pytest_runtest_call(item):
+def pytest_runtest_setup(item):
     outcome = yield
     raises_marker = item.get_marker('raises')
     if raises_marker:
@@ -33,15 +33,26 @@ def pytest_runtest_call(item):
                 except(ExpectedMessage):
                     excinfo = sys.exc_info()
                     if traceback:
-                        outcome.excinfo = excinfo[:2] + (traceback, )
+                        outcome._excinfo = excinfo[:2] + (traceback, )
                     else:
-                        outcome.excinfo = excinfo
+                        outcome._excinfo = excinfo
+        elif raised_exception:
+            try:
+                raise ExpectedException('Expected exception {}, but got {} with message: {}'.format(
+                    exception, type(raised_exception), raised_exception
+                ))
+            except ExpectedException:
+                excinfo = sys.exc_info()
+                if traceback:
+                    outcome._excinfo = excinfo[:2] + (traceback, )
+                else:
+                    outcome._excinfo = excinfo
         else:
             try:
-                raise raised_exception or ExpectedException('Expected exception {}, but it did not raise'.format(exception))
-            except(ExpectedException):
+                raise ExpectedException('Expected exception {}, but it did not raise'.format(exception))
+            except ExpectedException:
                 excinfo = sys.exc_info()
                 if traceback:
-                    outcome.excinfo = excinfo[:2] + (traceback, )
+                    outcome._excinfo = excinfo[:2] + (traceback, )
                 else:
-                    outcome.excinfo = excinfo
+                    outcome._excinfo = excinfo
```

Hopefully this all will be useful to somebody some day if they ever have such specialized needs!  @Authentise thanks for making `pytest_raises` available, with an agreeable license, so that I can include this hacked together stuff directly in my library (with proper attribution of course xD).

Alright, so if we decide to use the branching model and support just the latest version of Satellite, which is totally fine, we should also consider how the version and releases of NailGun will be handled. We have some other tools like the Ansible module written by @ehelms and other tools that may be using NailGun and dropping that support by requiring the users to clone an specific branch and not install from PyPI seems not good to me.

I know that Robottelo is the great customer we have for NailGun but I am making sure that every decision made in the past are not just dropped because it will be easy to maintain without even thinking about them.

In my opinion dropping the versioning checking and bringing that to the git logic (using branches) have the same effort than dealing with if/else blocks. I am saying that because basically instead of having a new if block we will have another branch and that may require cherry picks which can make that also not easy to maintain.

So all this is just a matter of making a design decision, my vote is still for having NailGun to support many Satellite versions within the same installation.

This is simply a modified `test_letsencrypt_auto_certonly_standalone.sh` test, except it uses the local copy of `letsencrypt-auto`.

Doing the simple update of `letsencrypt-auto-requirements.txt` to `cryptography` v1.2.3 causes failures on most Red Hat systems. The problem is `cryptography` v1.2.3 requires `setuptools>=1.0` ([source](https://github.com/pyca/cryptography/blob/1.2.3/setup.py#L40)) which wasn't required in our previously pinned version of `cryptography` ([source](https://github.com/pyca/cryptography/blob/1.1.2/setup.py#L40)). A log of test farm failures can be found [here](https://gist.github.com/f44592b1054ea99c719b).

Next I tried pinning `setuptools` to the top of `letsencrypt-auto-requirements.txt` and got errors like [this](https://gist.github.com/830ef32edec5ef5eeb46) on some systems.

Finally, I split `setuptools` into its own requirements file and all tests finally pass. This change is ugly, but it successfully fixes the problem. Okay, I see what you're saying. This is caused by the logic that determines what packages we do or don't have, and when to fall back. `find_packages` filters packages by their name (not their version): ```python def find_packages(pkgs, prefix=""): prefix = normalize_pkgname(prefix) for x in pkgs: if prefix and x.pkgname_norm != prefix: continue yield x ``` and the fallback is determined by whether or not `find_packages()` returns anything: ``` @app.route("/simple/:prefix/") @auth("list") def simple(prefix=""): # PEP 503: require normalized prefix normalized = core.normalize_pkgname(prefix) if prefix != normalized: return redirect('/simple/{0}/'.format(normalized), 301) files = sorted(core.find_packages(packages(), prefix=prefix), key=lambda x: (x.parsed_version, x.relfn)) if not files: if config.redirect_to_fallback: return redirect("%s/%s/" % (config.fallback_url.rstrip("/"), prefix)) return HTTPError(404) fp = request.fullpath links = [(os.path.basename(f.relfn), urljoin(fp, "../../packages/%s" % f.fname_and_hash(config.hash_algo))) for f in files] tmpl = """\ Links for {{prefix}}

Links for {{prefix}}

% for file, href in links: {{file}}
% end """ return template(tmpl, prefix=prefix, links=links) ``` I'm not sure that this isn't intentional, because if you're specifying the pypiserver as the only index, and you have placed a package of a given version there, it seems like it would be reasonable to assume that your intention is to only have *that* version available, to prevent clients from downloading, for example, a bad version from PyPI. I'd like to think about this a bit and look through old issues. I'm open to feedback, but I can definitely see the rationale in *not* falling back in this case. If we decided to keep it the way it is, I do agree that the documentation should be clearer. `disable redirect to real PyPI index for packages not found in the local index` could definitely be explicit in mentioning that the fallback is not triggered when a package (but not its specific version) isn't found in the index. Thoughts? As a workaround, you can of course add that version to your index, or specify pypi.python.org as an extra index URL in your pip command.

Sorry to hear you're having issues! 

I'm not sure if you've copied the `pip` command exactly, but if you're running it as written you might run into problems because `pip` is actually trying to download that URL as a package, rather than using at as an index. Could you ensure that you're running it like this?

`pip3 install --index-url http://pypi.[mydomain].net:8080 package-name`

If that works, you can set it so that you don't have to include it on the command line using a config file or environment variables for pip ([docs](https://pip.pypa.io/en/stable/user_guide/#config-file)).

If that doesn't work, see below!

On the server where you're running `pypiserver`, do you have the logs going to a file or stdout? Do you see any errors in the logs there?

If not, could you run the server with the `-vvv` argument and see if anything interesting shows up when you're making a request?

It would also be useful to know what the setup is for the server. Is it serving packages directly, or is it behind `nginx`? What command are you using to run the server?

@achourasia i was thinking about this over the weekend.  I think the above description is actually outside the scope of the folium project, if you wanting to use flask to serve data via a map interface in the above described way i would actually skip folium and go straight to a JS leaflet map/UI with a flask backend, leveraging a geo json/jsonp response. Folium is great for simple map plotting but you are talking about some serious data.

There is however twos ways you could do it in folium. 
1) Add two buttons, one to go forward in time, one to go back, these time are from an array list. These buttons call a function to create a folium map with the geojson, or update the map with a new geojson. And thus when you change time using the buttons a different geojson gets loaded. 

2) Add all the layers to the folium map, then make it possible to only select one from the map controls (similar to the wms layers), then hide the other ones.

the above two ways are a bit of a hack, and it would actually be easier to just do this in leaflet and JS...

> If problem is with tmux thinking that U+231A is wider then GNOME terminal thinks

This wouldn't explain the current problem. Then tmux would print one less character (because it thinks one is wider) and hence it wouldn't fill up the entire width. I think it is the opposite: tmux thinks it's a regular character, while gnome-terminal thinks it's a double wide. Hence it overflows.

As stated at https://bugzilla.gnome.org/show_bug.cgi?id=762052#c30, gnome-terminal (vte) uses g_unichar_iswide() instead of wcwidth() (which is used by xterm and probably tmux as well).

Could you please try these:

    echo $'\u231A' | wc -L

This prints glibc's wcwidth(), I assume the output will be 1.

    echo ABCDE; echo a$'\u231A'cde

Do the uppercase and lowercase letter align properly? I guess that in xterm they do, whereas in gnome-terminal they don't (the watch occupies 2 cells).

What metadata exactly? For the filename we could just encrypt the actual filename that is stored on B2 and use base64 or the like to have printable characters. One issue is that this increases the length of filenames by at least 33%, so filenames that are already close to 250 bytes don't fit anymore. Not sure if this might be a problem for some users. But this is the only method I came up with that would allow easy searching for filenames, without decrypting the name of all uploaded files first.

I'm going to put a vote in favor of excluding dump-only fields when they're passed in. The dump-only field is "known," it's just a one-way sort of deal.

Also, if dump-only fields aren't excluded, it makes `unknown=INCLUDE` essentially override (without any validation) `dump_only=True`. This is particularly troublesome since `unknown` is declared on the Schema, and `dump_only` is declared on a field; specific (a field parameter) should take precedence over general (a schema parameter).

Of course, it would be _really_ nice to have a way to configure this, so we can each have our own opinions, but maybe that's ugly.

- [ ] The doc strings should follow the pvlib/numpydoc convention. (I know it's not your favorite, but I think consistency is important.)
- [ ] The ``est`` in ``est_voc`` should be expanded to ``estimate``.
- [ ] pvlib-python is not entirely consistent regarding ``voc`` vs. ``v_oc`` and similar. The keys/columns of the returned dicts/dataframes use ``v_oc``, while the module specifications use ``voc``. I prefer ``v_oc``, so my preference for the function name would be ``estimate_v_oc``.
- [ ] ``vd`` should probably be ``diode_voltage``.
- [ ] Maybe the older functions should be moved into ``singlediode_methods.py``?
- [ ] I like the name ``bishop88``. Can we replace all instances of ``fast*`` and ``slow*`` with something similar (``newton``, etc.) ? While perhaps descriptive now, fast/slow runs the risk of becoming problematic in the future (``faster*``, ``faster_still*``, etc).

The fix for this case is simple. The 2.0 Patterning Language spec defines the start literal as `START`. In the current ANTLR grammar it's `[Ss][Tt][Aa][Rr][Tt]`. It should only be `START`, based on the 2.0 spec where text highlighted in light blue is a literal. There's no need to allow `StArt` or other permutations.

I'd like to explain why such errors happen with SQLite. Lets consider the following model for example:

```
class Model(models.Model):
    field = MoneyField(max_digits=10, decimal_places=2)
```

And lets rename "field" to "new_field".

Django creates a new table with new fields and then inserts all data from the old table into it.
For field renaming Django creates 2 operations - rename "amount" field, and rename "currency" field.

During "amount" field renaming a new table is created, in our case it will contain the following fields:

- id
- field_currency
- new_field
- new_field_currency

"new_field_currency" is added because of current MoneyField behaviour - it will always add a currency field with `_currency` suffix (by default). But we don't need it for renaming "field" to "new_field".

To move data to the new table Django will execute the following SQL (roughly):

```SQL
INSERT INTO money_app_model (id, new_field, field_currency) SELECT id, field, field_currency FROM money_app_model__old
```

And since "new_field_currency" is not NULL this query will fail with "NOT NULL constraint failed" error.

#345 fixes it by simple checking if "amount" field already has "currency" field attached.
